dialog: Dad? It’s me... Are you there? Dad?
I’ve just had her on the phone.
You can’t go on behaving like this.
It’s my flat, isn’t it? I mean, this is incredible. You burst in on me as if... I’ve no idea who she is, this woman. I never asked her for anything.
To help me do what? I don’t need her. I don’t need anyone.
She told me you’d called her a little bitch. And I don’t know what else.
What, just because I called her a...
No. She told me you threatened her. Physically.
Physically? Me? Obviously she has no idea what she’s talking about. This woman is raving mad, Anne. Best if she does leave, believe me.
Listen... I didn’t want to tell you... but if you must know, I suspect she was...
Angela? Of course not. What are you talking about?
I’m telling you. She stole my watch.
Isn’t it more likely you just lost it?
No, no, no. I already had my suspicions. So I set a trap for her. I left my watch somewhere, out in the open, to see if she’d pinch it.
Where? Where did you leave it?
Mm? Somewhere. Can’t remember. All I know is it’s nowhere to be found. That girl stole it. I know she did.
I don’t know what to do.
That’s what we’re doing, isn’t it?
I mean, seriously. This is the third one you’ve...
I said, I don’t need her! I don’t need her or anyone else! I can manage very well on my own!
She wasn’t easy to find, you know. It’s not that easy. I thought she was really good. A lot of good qualities. She... and now she doesn’t want to work here any more.
You’re not listening to what I’m telling you. This girl stole my watch! I’m not going to live with a thief.
Have you looked in your bathroom cupboard?
In your bathroom cupboard. Behind the boiler. Where you hide your valuables.
Have you been in my cupboard? Anne, tell me the truth.
Then how do you know that... I mean... that I sometimes... with my valuables...
I can’t remember. I must have opened it by accident.
Where are you going? I didn’t touch anything, Dad.
You realise Angela had nothing to do with it.

Only because I hid it. Luckily. Just in time! Otherwise, I’d be sitting here talking to you with no means of knowing what time it was. It’s five o’clock, if you’re interested. Myself, I am interested. Pardon me for breathing.

Yes. But why are you...? You keep looking at me as if there was something wrong. Everything’s fine, Anne. The world is turning. You’ve always been that way. A worrier. Like your mother. Your mother was always scared. Always looking for reasons to be scared. Whereas your sister has always been much more... At least she doesn't keep badgering me.
Where is she, by the way? Have you heard from her?
I’m going to have to move, Dad.
I’m going to have to leave London.
We talked about this. Do you remember?
Is that why you’re so keen on this nurse living with me? Well, obviously it is. The rats are leaving the ship.
I won’t be here, Dad. I won’t be able to come here everyday. You need to understand that.
You’re leaving? When? I mean... why?
No, it’s just that... since your... What was his name?
That’s right. You have to admit, since James, there hasn’t been a lot of... What’s he do, anyway?
He lives in Paris. I’m going to go and live there.
What, you? In Paris? You’re not going to do that, are you, Anne? I mean, wake up... They don’t even speak English.
So, if I understand correctly, you’re leaving me. Is that it? You’re abandoning me...
What’s going to become of me?
You know, it’s important to me. Otherwise, I wouldn’t be going. I... I really love him.
I’ll come back and see you often. At weekends. But I can’t leave you here all on your own. It’s not possible. That’s why. If you refuse to have a carer, I’m going to have to...
You’re going to have to what?
Anne... you’re going to have to what?
What are you doing here? What are you doing in my flat?
Don’t you recognise me? It’s me, Paul.
Who? What are you doing here?
You live in my flat? That’s the best yet. What is this nonsense?
I... I’m going to phone Anne.
Thank you, yes, I do know who Anne is! Do you know her? You a friend of hers?
I’m speaking to you. Do you know Anne?
Ah, yes. Of course. Yes, yes. Obviously. Ten years already? Time passes at such a lick... But I thought... Didn’t you, aren’t you separated?
Are you sure? I mean... I mean, are you sure ?
But this thing about France? Wasn’t she supposed to be going to Paris to... wasn’t she?
No, it’s just your father isn’t feeling very well. I think he’d like to see you.
Fine, but don’t be too long.
She’s just doing some shopping. She’ll be up in a minute.
She told me she was going to go and live in Paris. She told me the other day.
What was she going to do in Paris?

Yes, she has. She told me the other day. I’m not an idiot. She told me she was moving. To go and live with him. I even remember telling her it was a stupid idea because, you know, they don’t even speak English. You don’t know about this?
Have I put my foot in it?
I’ve put my foot in it.
No, no, don’t worry. She hasn’t mentioned it to me, but I’m sure she was intending to...
You didn’t know anything about the Frenchman?
Never mind. Chin up. Anyway, they all end up leaving sooner or later. I speak from experience.
You want something to drink while we’re waiting? Glass of water? Fruit juice?
No, but I mean... What was I going to say?
It’s because of that girl... That nurse...
I’ve forgotten her name. That girl your wife insists on handing me over to. A nurse. You know about this? As if I wasn’t able to manage on my own...
She told me I needed the help of this... When I can manage perfectly well on my own. Even if she does have to go abroad.
I don’t understand why she persists in... Look at me. Take a good look at me...
That’s right, Paul. Take a good look at me. I can still manage on my own. Don’t you think? I’m not completely...

You agree? Of course you agree. But her? I don’t know where this stupid obsession comes from. She’s always been that way. Ever since she was little. Thing is, she’s not very bright. Not very... you agree? Not very intelligent. She gets that from her mother.
I think she tries to do the best she can for you, Anthony.

The best she can, the best she can... I never asked her for anything. I don’t know what she’s cooking up against me. But she’s cooking something up. She’s cooking something up, that I do know. I suspect she wants to put me in a home for...
Yes, I’ve seen the signs. But let me make something absolutely clear: I’m not leaving my flat! I’m not leaving it!
If you remember, you moved here, I mean you moved to our place while you were waiting for...
For a new carer. Because you quarrelled with the last one... With Angela.
Yes. Don’t you remember? That’s why you’re staying in our place. While you wait.
Yes, Paul... So you’re telling me, I’m in your place.
Nothing much. Your father seemed a bit... confused.
Something wrong? Are you all right, Dad?
I’m here, Dad... I went downstairs to do some shopping. And now I’m back.
I... I see, but... What did you buy?
A chicken. Sound good? Are you hungry?
Here, let me have it. I’ll go and fix everything.
Paul said you weren’t feeling very well.
I was in the kitchen... Making a cup of tea... I was alone in the flat... Suddenly, I heard a sound... So I came in here and there was your husband...
Well, yours, my dear. Not mine.
I got divorced more than five years ago. Have you forgotten?
Are you doing this on purpose? I’m talking about... him. Who just left with the chicken.
The chicken? What are you on about, Dad?
Right here, just a minute ago. Did you not hand over a chicken to someone?
The chicken! A minute ago you were holding a chicken, were you not? A chicken. A CHICKEN!
What chicken? What are you talking about, Dad?
But he was here just now!
I think you’re mistaken, Dad. There’s no one here.
Who? The man with the chicken?
Your husband. The man with the chicken. Why are you smiling?
All this nonsense is driving me crazy.
There’s something funny going on. Believe me, Anne, there’s something funny going on!
Come and sit down. Come on...
Now don’t worry. Everything’ll sort itself out. Mm?
Yes, it will. Don’t worry. Have you taken your medication?
What’s that got to do with anything?
Let’s give you your medication. The evening dose. Then you’ll feel better.

It’s been going on for some time. Strange things going on around us. Haven’t you noticed? There was this man claiming this wasn’t my flat. A really unsympathetic-looking man. A bit like your husband. Only worse. In my flat, you understand what I’m saying? It’s the best yet. Don’t you think? In my flat. He told me...
But... this is my flat, isn’t it? Mm? Anne... This is my flat?
Tell me, Anne, this really is my flat, isn’t it?
Yes, I know... I know... No. You can’t imagine how difficult it is sometimes... The other day, he didn’t even recognise me... I know...
No, I’m expecting her any minute. Yes...
Right, right. I’ll call you back. Lots of love. Thanks...
Hello. Not too early, am I?
No, no. Not at all. Come in. Come in.
Come in. Thanks for coming round.
My father’s in his room. Would you like something to drink?
So, yes, as I was telling you, I... He’s a bit upset by the whole idea of...
Yes. And that can cause him to... Anyway, I think he’s a bit annoyed with me. I’m telling you this just to warn you he’s capable of reacting... unexpectedly.
Has he lived on his own up to now?
Yes. In a flat, not too far from here. It worked. I was able to look in on him practically every day. But eventually, we’ve had to come to another arrangement. It wasn’t viable any more.

He’s had several carers one after the other. But he’s had difficulty getting on with them. He has his ways... That’s why I moved him here, in with me. But I can’t manage him on my own. It’s too much for me. I have to work and... That’s why I...
Well, that’s why I need someone to help me.
You did... Dad, I’d like you to meet Laura.
I explained to you that Laura was going to come by today so you could meet.
But I... Don’t we know each other?
Are you sure? I’ve a definite impression I’ve seen you before...
Well. So, Laura’s come by to see us to get a bit of an idea of how you live and to see to what extent she might be able to help you.
I know, dear, I know that. You’ve already told me a hundred times.
My daughter has a tendency to repeat herself. You know what it’s like... it’s an age thing...
Would you like something to drink?
You’re very kind, but no thanks.
Sure? An aperitif? Must be about time for an aperitif, isn’t it? What time is it? It’s...
Hang on, I’ll be right back.
He’s gone to look for his watch.
Just as I thought, time for an aperitif. I have two watches. I’ve always had two. One on my wrist and the other in my head. It’s always been that way. Now, would you like something, young lady?
What? I’m allowed to offer our guest something, aren’t I?
What are you going to have?
I’m not offering you one, Anne.
Never. Not a drop. That’s why she seems so...
Sober. Her mother was the same. Her mother was the... soberest woman I’ve ever met. Whereas her little sister... That was quite another story.
That’s right. Even though I hardly ever hear from the other one. Lucy. All the same, she was always my favorite.
Do you ever hear from her? I don’t understand why she never gets in touch. Never. Dazzling girl. A painter. Look. Beautiful, isn’t it?
I’d give everything I own for a glass of whisky. Don’t you agree?
Well, I don’t own all that much...
Don’t you? What do you do for a living?
Well, I... I look after... other people.
Yes. My job is to help people who need help.
Sounds like one of those girls you’re always trying to dump off on me.
Must be a difficult job, isn’t it? Spending all day with some...
Am I right? I couldn’t stand it.
What about you, what did you do for a living?
What do you know about it?
Why? Can’t you imagine me as a tap dancer?
Of course. It’s just... I’ve always loved tap dancing.
You as well? I’m still great at it. I’ll show you...
It’s nothing. Sorry. Sorry. It’s just... the whisky.
That’s it, I know. I know who you remind me of.
Lucy. Lucy, when she was her age.
My other daughter. Don’t you agree?
Her unbearable habit of laughing inanely.
I had you there, didn’t I? Ha ha.

You see, the situation’s very simple. I’ve been living in this flat... oh, for a long time now. I’m extremely attached to it. I bought it more than thirty years ago. Can you imagine? You weren’t even born. It’s a big flat. And my daughter is very interested in it.

Let me explain. My daughter is of the opinion that I can’t manage on my own. So she’s moved in with me. Ostensibly to help me. With this man she met not long ago, just after her divorce, who has a very bad influence on her, I have to tell you.
Look, what are you talking about, Dad?
And now she’d like to convince me that I can’t manage on my own.

But it’s not going to happen that way. I may as well tell you. I have no intention of leaving any time soon. No, you heard me. I intend to outlive you. Both of you. Yes. Well, I don’t know about you... But my daughter, yes. I shall make a point of it. I’m going to inherit from her. Not the other way round. The day of her funeral, I shall give a little speech to remind everyone how heartless and manipulative she was.
Why? She understands completely. You’re the one who doesn’t understand.

I’ve been trying to explain to her for months that I can manage very well on my own. But she refuses to listen. I don’t need any help from anyone and I will not leave my flat. All I want is for everyone to bugger off!
Having said that, it was a great pleasure, I’ll be leaving you.
When you said he had his ways, you weren’t kidding...
Don’t be. That sort of reaction is quite normal.
Mm? Yes, yes... I think so. She said she’d start tomorrow.
Yes. Then we’ll see how the first day goes. I was so afraid it wasn’t going to work. But in the end it was fine. He was charming.
He turned on the charm for her... You should have seen it... He told her he’d been a dancer. A tap dancer.

Yes. She started to laugh. Not in a mean way, you understand. There was something kind about her. I was relieved. I don’t know how to describe it to you. As if she was going to be able to... Well, as if the two of them were going to get on really well...
He said she reminded him of Lucy.
Oh, yes? How old is she?
I don’t know. Thirty. Something like that.
What’s the matter with you? If it went well, that’s good news, isn’t it?
So? What’s the matter with you? Tell me.
Just now... When I came back with the dinner... He didn’t recognise me... I... I don’t know. It did something to me.
Come. Let me give you a hug.
I saw it in his eyes. He didn’t recognise me. Not at all. I was like a stranger to him.
You have to get used to it.
I think you can, I think you’re managing very well. Come here...
Ah, Dad! Dinner’ll be ready in five minutes. That suit you?
Very good, dear. Suits me fine. Suits me... But... Hello.
Yes, yes. But... we have guests this evening?
Everything all right? Had a good day?
Very good. Laura came by. Didn’t she, Dad? Laura came to see us just now.
The young woman who came to see us just now.
And we went to see the doctor...
Has anybody seen my watch? Can’t seem to find it.
I’ve been looking for it for some time.
You must have put it in your cupboard. Don’t you think? In your hiding-place...
What are you talking about, Anne? I really don’t know what you’re talking about. What cupboard? Mm? There’s no cupboard. No cupboard.
Have you looked in your cupboard?
I’ve just come from there. It’s not there. I must have lost it somewhere. Or else it’s been stolen.
What do you mean, “No, it hasn’t”? The watch must be somewhere! It can’t have flown away!
You want me to go and look?

Very much so. If it’s not a bother. Because it’s a worry. I’m worried. I am losing all my things, everyone’s just helping themselves. If this goes on much longer, I’ll be stark naked. And I won’t even know what time it is.
So what time is it? Exactly.
That late? Shouldn’t we be sitting down to dinner?
Yes. As soon as the chicken’s ready. In five minutes.
Yes. The one Anne just bought.
It’s pretty, your watch. It’s... It’s pretty. It’s... Is it yours? I mean, is it yours?
So. Apparently it went very well.
Well, your meeting with... the carer.
Oh? Yes. Very well. Very well.
I’ve no idea, I’ve never seen her.
No, it... It went well. Anne seemed pleased. You know, it’s mainly for her. I don’t really need... I mean, it’s mainly for Anne. Might I have a look at it? Your watch...
You’re right, it’s important for her that this works out. She’s been worried about you, you know. It makes her very unhappy when you fall out with... What is it about my watch?
Nothing. I was just looking... I want to check if... It’s pretty. Very pretty. Did you buy it?
No, I mean... Was it a present or did you buy it?
I don’t suppose you kept the receipt...

I don’t know why, we never really got on. Whereas her little sister, that was quite another story... Do you know her? My other daughter? Now she, she’s marvellous. I haven’t seen her for months. I can’t blame her. She’s traveling round the world, I think. She’s a painter.

But I’d be so happy if she came to see me one day. I’d take her in my arms and we’d be glued to one another for hours on end, like we used to be a long time ago, when she was little and she still used to call me “little daddy”, “little daddy”. That’s what she used to call me. Nice, isn’t it, “little daddy”?
Now, I’d like to ask you something.
But I want an honest answer. Nothing fancy... Can you do that for me?
How much longer do you intend to hang around getting on everybody’s tits?
Have you done something to your hair?
You’re living with your daughter at the moment, is that right?
Yes. Until she goes to live in Paris.
No, Dad, why do you keep going on about Paris?
You keep changing your mind. How do you expect people to keep up?
But there was never any question of going to Paris, Dad.
Yes, there was. You told me.
I’m sorry, Anne. You told me the other day. Have you forgotten?
She’s forgotten. Listen, Anne, I think you’re starting to suffer from memory loss. I should have a word with the doctor, if I were you...
In any event, I’m not going to Paris.
Well, good. I mean, Paris, they don’t even speak English.
What’s the matter? What? All right... I’ll be up in a minute.
Nothing much... Your father... I think he’d like to see you...
Right. I’ll go and get the dinner ready...
We have to find another arrangement.
Why are you saying this to me today? I mean, when tomorrow morning... there’s this girl starting and...

Yes. You’re right. We’ll see. Maybe it’ll work very well with this girl. You seem to think she’s good. But, believe me, the doctor is right, the moment will come when… However good she is... He’s ill,  Anne. He’s ill.
Dad. What are you doing, standing there? Come and sit down. Come on.
Yes. It went very well. Don’t you agree, Dad?
You made her laugh a lot.
Yes. She told me she thought you were charming. That you had your ways, but that you were charming. She’s coming back tomorrow morning. To start working here.
I would. It’s good, this chicken. Don’t you think? Where’d you buy it?
Is she doing full days? I mean...
You have a daughter who looks after you properly. Don’t you? You’re lucky.
Anne? She’s tired. Needs a bit of sun.
You need to look after her, old man. Why don’t you go away somewhere?
Why? You want me to tell you why?
Sometimes I wonder if you’re doing it on purpose.
We had planned to go to Italy ten days ago.
Yes. But we had to cancel it at the last minute. You know why?
Because of your row with Angela.
The woman who was looking after you. Before Laura. Have you forgotten?
We weren’t able to go and leave you on your own. We had to cancel our holiday and bring you over here. And now it seems you’re going to stay here. For good. If I understand correctly...
Not at all, Anne. I think I’m being very patient. Very patient. Believe me.
What are you trying to say?
Yes, you are, tell me. Why are you telling me how patient you are?
Anyone else would have pressured you to...
To do what the situation calls for.
Where’s the chicken? Did you take the chicken away?
Yes. Did you want some more?
Yes. Is it in the kitchen?
I’ll go and fetch it for you.
No, it’s all right, I’ll go.
Why do you say things like that in front of him?
Listen... I totally understand your feelings.

I do... What I don’t understand is... I mean, you do so much for him. I respect you for that. You took the decision to bring him here. And why not? But... How can I put this? I honestly think you ought to come up with a different solution... He’s completely lost it, Anne.
How do you want me to talk?
I’m telling the truth. We have to find another arrangement.
Why are you saying this to me today? I mean, when tomorrow morning... there’s this girl starting and...
Yes, you’re right. We’ll see. Maybe it’ll work very well with this girl. You seem to think she’s good.
But, believe me, the doctor is right, the moment will come when... However good she is... He’s ill, Anne. He’s ill.
Dad. What are you doing, standing there? Come and sit down. Come on.
Dad? Are you up already? Did you sleep well?
Shall I get you some coffee before she arrives?
You’re mixing this up with your flat. There’s never been a painting here. Come and have your breakfast.
She’ll be back soon. At the end of the day. I’m going to look for your medication.
I’ll be back. I’m just going to look for your medication.
Time for your medication. Here we are. Best to take them now. Then it’s done. Don’t you think? There are three today. This little blue one...
That’s the one you like. Your little blue pill. Look, it’s a pretty colour, isn’t it?
Can I ask you a question?
Then why are you speaking to me as if I were retarded?
“Your little blue pill.” “Your little blue pill.”
I’m sorry. I didn’t think you...
It’s really unpleasant. You’ll see when you get to my age. Which’ll happen sooner than you think, by the way.
I apologise. I... It won’t happen again.
What do you think? About my flat!
Yes. These chairs, for instance. There. Who put them there?
I don’t know. Your daughter, I imagine.
Obviously. My daughter... Obviously... All the same, it’s extraordinary! Not even to ask my opinion. I... Do you know what’s being planned? For this flat?
Well, I do. I keep my eyes open. I know everything.
By the way, I wanted to apologise if I was a little... Last time we met...
No problem. Your daughter warned me. She told me you had your ways.
It’s amazing how like Lucy you look. My other daughter. Not Anne, no. The other one. The one I love.
Anne told me what happened to her. I’m sorry. I didn’t know.
Are you taking your medication? Then we’ll go and get dressed.
You’re speaking to me as if I were retarded.
“And then we’ll go and get dressed...” “Your little blue pill.”
Thing is, I’m very intelligent. You need to bear that in mind, d’you understand?
Yes, I’ll... bear it in mind.
It’s true. I’m very intelligent... Sometimes, I even surprise myself. Memory like an elephant.
Oh, yes, so I did... What are they doing there?
I’ll go and get you another glass of water.
No, no. Don’t bother. I’ll swallow them with... You’ll see. With the coffee.
You’ll see. Are you watching? Watch carefully. I stick them in my gob. Watch, there they go, hey presto, they’re in my mouth. Did you see? Did you see? Did you see?
Good. And now, the coffee. Watch carefully... Hey presto.
I worked in the circus a bit when I was young.
Yes. I was quite talented. Especially at conjuring tricks. Would you like me to show you a little magic?
What’s the point? I’ll only have to put my pyjamas back on tonight, won’t I? Might as well save some time.
I see what you mean. But if you stay in your pyjamas, we won’t be able to go out.
The park. It’s a nice day.
Fine. We were going to get dressed.
I just wanted a word with you. In fact.
In that case, I’ll... I’ll go and get your things ready.
Don’t leave me on my own.
I’ll be in the next room. I’ll be right back.
Can I ask you a question?
But I want an honest answer. Nothing fancy... Can you do that for me?
Well, then... how much longer do you intend to hang around getting on everybody’s tits?
Yes, you. I’d like to know your opinion.
I mean, do you intend to go on ruining your daughter’s life? Or is it too much to hope that you’ll behave reasonably in the foreseeable future?
But... What are you talking about?
About you, Anthony. About you. Your attitude.
What are you doing? I can’t allow this.
Suppose I do it again, then what will you do?
You’ll have to take me on. Physically.
Are you saying that to tempt me? See, me as well, there’s something I can’t allow. Getting on everybody’s tits. Past a certain age.
Stop it! Do you hear me? Stop this at once.
Yes. I won’t put up with that. I find that totally inappropriate.
Stop that! I told you to stop it!
Is it because of your watch? Dad, is that the reason? I found it, look! Shush. Come on, don’t cry.
You’ll be all right now. Mm? Shush. You’ll be all right. You’ll be all right. Let’s eat our chicken. Shall we? You like chicken, don’t you?
It’s seven o’clock. Time to eat.
But I thought it was morning. I’ve only just got up. Look, I’m still in my pyjamas.
No, it’s evening and I’ve cooked you a chicken. Come on, let’s eat. Come on. Little daddy. Little daddy.
Sit down. It’s all ready. Are you hungry?
You have a visitor today. Remember?
How could I forget? You never stop talking about it.
I dreamt about her last night.
Yes. Well, I think I did. I can see her face.
You know, she really reminded me of your sister...
Laura? Yes. That’s what you said yesterday.
Anyway, if you like her, I’m happy. She seems really nice. I mean, sweet. And efficient. She’ll look after you well.
Good. We’d better get you dressed before she arrives, don’t you think?
Laura. Your new carer. The one you like.
Better to have a jacket on when she arrives.
She very much enjoyed meeting you yesterday, you know. I must say you did quite a little number on her.
Yes. You convinced her you knew how to dance. That you were good at tap dancing.
She said she hoped you’d give her a demonstration. One day.
Funny. I didn’t even know I knew how to tap dance. Did you?
But... so soon? I’m not ready. I’m not dressed.
Never mind. You can get dressed later.
No. I... I have to put some trousers on, Anne. Anne, I’m not properly dressed.
Don’t leave me like this. What’s she going to think of me? I have to get dressed. Where are my clothes?
Dad. Why do you always make everything so difficult? You can get dressed later. There’s nothing to worry about.
I will. Look, I’m in my pyjamas. I have to put my trousers on.
Hello. Not too late, am I?
No, no. Not at all. Come in. Come in.
Here we are. We’re just getting dressed...
Would you like something to drink? Coffee?
I don’t want her. Where’s the one I like? Where is she?
But, Dad... what are you talking about? Say hello to Laura.
There’s something that doesn’t make sense about this. It doesn’t make sense!
Do you remember me? We met yesterday.
We were starting to get to know one another...
And I told you I’d come back... Just to see the way you did things and whether I could help you.
Dad? Can I talk to you ?
You remember at first, when you came to our place, it was... I mean, it was... a stop gap. Because you’d fallen out with Angela. But... I’m wondering if it wouldn’t be... better to...
What do you think of this room? It’s rather nice, isn’t it?
It looks on to the park.
It’s very nice. It’s like being in a hotel? Don’t you think?
That’s what all the residents say.
I think you might be better off here.
Here. I was wondering if it wouldn’t be more reassuring... nicer for you if we came to a joint decision that you should move in here.
What about you? What are you going to do? Where are you going to sleep? Which room?
If you remember, I’m going to go and live in Paris.
I am. Remember? I told you about it... Remember?
But you said... Are you sure?
You told me you were staying here... with me...
No, I have to go. It’s important. I already explained it to you. But I’ll come and see you. Occasional weekends.
What about your sister? Where’s she?
If you knew how much I missed her...
I do too, Dad, I miss her too. We all miss her.
I didn’t ask about the time. I asked you what I was doing here. Where’s Anne?
Look, I’ve brought you your medication.
Why don’t you just fuck off with your medication? What are you, a nurse?
Oh, you are... Oh, so that’s it... Oh, I see. You are a nurse.
Oh, I see. That’s what I was thinking. You’re the type. Typical nurse. So what are you doing here?
You don’t say! Looking after me? First I’ve heard of it. Since when?
For quite a few weeks now.
For quite a few weeks? I’m happy to hear it. Amazing! Nobody tells me anything in this house. But I thought we were getting a new one.
The one who looks a bit like Lucy. My other daughter.
I met her the other day. She came here... didn’t she?
She was supposed to start this morning. Laura. Wasn’t she?
I think you’re getting mixed up, Anthony.
The one who reminded me of Lucy...
Yes, all right, fine. Let’s take this medication. It’s not timed to the minute, is it?
Oh? Where is she? Has she gone out?
If you remember, she lives in Paris.
What? No, no, she thought about going. But in the end, it didn’t happen.
She’s been living there for several months.
My daughter? In Paris? No, listen, they don’t even speak English.
Look, yesterday, this postcard she sent you. We read it together. Don’t you remember?
I tell you this every day. She lives in Paris because she met a man called Paul, who she now lives with. But she comes to see you sometimes.

Yes. Occasionally she comes for the weekend. She comes here. You go for a walk in the park. She tells you about her new life, what she’s up to. The other day, she brought you some tea. Because you like tea.
Me? I detest tea. I only drink coffee.
Fine. We were just going to get dressed.
No reason. But... how shall I put this? What’s he doing here? I mean... in my flat. Do I know him?
Yes. He’s Bill. You see him every day.
Sorry to ask this, but... I mean, you... you... Who are you, exactly?
Catherine. That’s right. Yes, yes, yes. Catherine. And he’s Bill.
Right. Right. And... What about me?
Anthony? Nice name, Anthony... Don’t you think?
My mother gave it to me. I imagine. Did you know her?
She was so... She had very big eyes. It was... I can see her face now. I hope she’ll come and see me sometimes. Mummy. Do you think? You were saying she might come occasionally for the weekend...
No, Mummy. I... I want my mummy. I want my mummy. I want... I want to get out of here. Have someone come and fetch me.
I want my mummy. I want her to come and fetch me. I want to go back home.
What’s the matter with you? Anthony... Come here. Come to me. Tell me what the matter is...
I feel as if... I feel as if I’m losing all my leaves, one after another.
Your leaves? What are you talking about?

The branches! And the wind... I don’t understand what’s happening any more. Do you understand what’s happening? All this business about the flat? You don’t know where you can put your head down. I know where my watch is. On my wrist. That I do know. For the journey. If not, I wouldn’t know when I might have to...
First we’ll get dressed, shall we?
We’ll get dressed and then we’ll go for a walk in the park, shall we?

Good. All the trees. And the leaves. And then we’ll come back here and have something to eat. Then you’ll have a siesta. All right? And if you’re on form, we’ll take another little walk. In the park. Just the two of us. Because it’s a nice day. Isn’t it?
The sun’s out. We have to make the most of it. It doesn’t happen every day. It never lasts very long when the weather’s as good as this, does it? So let’s get dressed, is that all right?
Now. Don’t be a baby. Come on. Come with me. All right? Come on. Easy. Easy. Shush. Shush. You’ll be all right in a minute. You’ll be all right. Shush...
